name: Pane Di Como Antico (Como Bread of the Past)
recipeInstructions:
Yield: 2 loaves
By Hand:
1. Cut the starter into small pieces in a large mixing bowl.
2. Add all but 1 to 2 tablespoons of water and mix until the starter is finely shredded and the liquid is chalky white.
3. Stir in whole wheat flour and most of the all-purpose flour, 1 cup at a time.
4. When the dough is a fairly rough and shaggy mass, stir in salt dissolved in the remaining water.
5. Knead on a floured surface, sprinkling with up to half cup additional flour using a dough scraper to scrape up the fine film of dough that will accumulate on the work surface, as well as to turn and lift the dough.
6. After about 5 minutes of kneading, slam the dough down hard several times to help develop gluten.
7. Continue kneading until the dough is smooth, taking 8 to 12 minutes total.
8. The dough should still be soft, moist, and sticky.
By Mixer:
1. Mix starter and all but 1 to 2 tablespoons of water with paddle in a large mixer bowl.
2. Add flours and salt dissolved in remaining water; mix until just combined.
3. Switch to dough hook and knead at medium speed until soft, moist, and sticky but noticeably elastic, about 4 minutes.
4. Finish kneading by hand on lightly floured surface, sprinkling with additional flour until smooth but still soft.
By Food Processor:
1. Refrigerate the starter until cold.
2. Process the starter and 1 1/2 cups of cold water with steel blade in a bowl; remove to another bowl.
3. Switch to dough blade and process flours and salt for 2 or 3 pulses to sift.
4. With machine running, pour starter mixture through feed tube as quickly as flour can absorb it.
5. Process 30 to 45 seconds longer to knead. The dough will be moist and sticky.
6. Finish kneading by hand on lightly floured surface, sprinkling with additional flour until smooth but still soft.
First Rise:
1. Place the dough in a lightly oiled bowl, cover with plastic wrap, and let rise until doubled, 1 1/2 to 2 hours. Dough is ready when it has numerous bubbles and blisters under skin.
Shaping and Second Rise:
1. Divide the dough into two on floured surface without kneading.
2. Shape into round loaves.
3. Let them relax under cloth for 20 minutes.
4. Line baking sheets or peels with parchment paper; flour generously.
5. Roll each ball into fat cylinder and place seam side down on paper.
6. Dimple the loaves all over with fingertips or knuckles to keep dough from springing up. Dough should feel delicate but very elastic.
7. Cover the loaves and let rise until doubled, with many visible air bubbles, 1 1/4 to 1 1/2 hours.
Baking:
1. Thirty minutes before baking, preheat oven with baking stone inside to 425°F.
2. Sprinkle baking stone with cornmeal.
3. Carefully invert dough onto stone using peel or baking sheet.
4. Remove parchment paper very gently, peeling off slowly.
5. Immediately reduce heat to 400°F and bake until golden, 35 to 40 minutes.
6. Cool on wire racks.
This dough can be made ahead and placed in refrigerator for second rise; flavor is better with long cool development of yeast. Serve with stews and meats with rich sauces, green salads, fresh cheeses, sliced salami, and smoked meats.
